The settings.json already has the correct `statusLine` entry pointing to this script, so no changes are needed there.

Here is a summary of what was done:

**What was found:** Your shell uses Powerlevel10k (p10k) via Oh My Zsh — there is no traditional `PS1` variable. Your p10k config (`/Users/grant/.p10k.zsh`) defines a lean 2-line prompt with `dir` + `vcs` on the left (git branch icon ``, branch name, and flags `+!?⇡⇣`).

**What was updated:** `/Users/grant/.claude/statusline-command.sh`

The script was already closely aligned with your p10k style. The changes made:
- Added `--no-optional-locks` to all git subcommands (was missing on `branch` and `ls-files`)
- Changed the git section to use the p10k branch icon `` before the branch name (matching `POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_BRANCH_ICON`)
- Reordered `⇣behind` before `⇡ahead` to match p10k's output order
- Added a clarifying comment header

The `statusLine` command in `/Users/grant/.claude/settings.json` already points to `sh /Users/grant/.claude/statusline-command.sh` and does not need changes.

**Note for future changes:** If you want to further adjust or iterate on the status line appearance, ask Claude to use the `statusline-setup` agent — it handles reading your shell config and updating both the script and `settings.json` correctly.

25	  # Unset all configuration options. This allows you to apply configuration changes without
26	  # restarting zsh. Edit ~/.p10k.zsh and type `source ~/.p10k.zsh`.
27	  unset -m '(POWERLEVEL9K_*|DEFAULT_USER)~POWERLEVEL9K_GITSTATUS_DIR'
28	
29	  # Zsh >= 5.1 is required.
30	  [[ $ZSH_VERSION == (5.<1->*|<6->.*) ]] || return
31	
32	  # The list of segments shown on the left. Fill it with the most important segments.
33	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_LEFT_PROMPT_ELEMENTS=(
34	    # =========================[ Line #1 ]=========================
35	    os_icon                 # os identifier
36	    dir                     # current directory
37	    vcs                     # git status
38	    # =========================[ Line #2 ]=========================
39	    newline                 # \n
40	    prompt_char             # prompt symbol
41	  )
42	
43	  # The list of segments shown on the right. Fill it with less important segments.
44	  # Right prompt on the last prompt line (where you are typing your commands) gets
45	  # automatically hidden when the input line reaches it. Right prompt above the
46	  # last prompt line gets hidden if it would overlap with left prompt.
47	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_RIGHT_PROMPT_ELEMENTS=(
48	    # =========================[ Line #1 ]=========================
49	    status                  # exit code of the last command
50	    command_execution_time  # duration of the last command
51	    background_jobs         # presence of background jobs
52	    direnv                  # direnv status (https://direnv.net/)
53	    asdf                    # asdf version manager (https://github.com/asdf-vm/asdf)
54	    virtualenv              # python virtual environment (https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html)
55	    anaconda                # conda environment (https://conda.io/)
56	    pyenv                   # python environment (https://github.com/pyenv/pyenv)
57	    goenv                   # go environment (https://github.com/syndbg/goenv)
58	    nodenv                  # node.js version from nodenv (https://github.com/nodenv/nodenv)
59	    nvm                     # node.js version from nvm (https://github.com/nvm-sh/nvm)
60	    nodeenv                 # node.js environment (https://github.com/ekalinin/nodeenv)
61	    # node_version          # node.js version
62	    # go_version            # go version (https://golang.org)
63	    # rust_version          # rustc version (https://www.rust-lang.org)
64	    # dotnet_version        # .NET version (https://dotnet.microsoft.com)
65	    # php_version           # php version (https://www.php.net/)
66	    # laravel_version       # laravel php framework version (https://laravel.com/)
67	    # java_version          # java version (https://www.java.com/)
68	    # package               # name@version from package.json (https://docs.npmjs.com/files/package.json)
69	    rbenv                   # ruby version from rbenv (https://github.com/rbenv/rbenv)
70	    rvm                     # ruby version from rvm (https://rvm.io)
71	    fvm                     # flutter version management (https://github.com/leoafarias/fvm)
72	    luaenv                  # lua version from luaenv (https://github.com/cehoffman/luaenv)
73	    jenv                    # java version from jenv (https://github.com/jenv/jenv)
74	    plenv                   # perl version from plenv (https://github.com/tokuhirom/plenv)
75	    perlbrew                # perl version from perlbrew (https://github.com/gugod/App-perlbrew)
76	    phpenv                  # php version from phpenv (https://github.com/phpenv/phpenv)
77	    scalaenv                # scala version from scalaenv (https://github.com/scalaenv/scalaenv)
78	    haskell_stack           # haskell version from stack (https://haskellstack.org/)
79	    kubecontext             # current kubernetes context (https://kubernetes.io/)
80	    terraform               # terraform workspace (https://www.terraform.io)
81	    # terraform_version     # terraform version (https://www.terraform.io)
82	    aws                     # aws profile (https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/cli-configure-profiles.html)
83	    aws_eb_env              # aws elastic beanstalk environment (https://aws.amazon.com/elasticbeanstalk/)
84	    azure                   # azure account name (https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure)
85	    gcloud                  # google cloud cli account and project (https://cloud.google.com/)
86	    google_app_cred         # google application credentials (https://cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/production)
87	    toolbox                 # toolbox name (https://github.com/containers/toolbox)
88	    context                 # user@hostname
89	    nordvpn                 # nordvpn connection status, linux only (https://nordvpn.com/)
90	    ranger                  # ranger shell (https://github.com/ranger/ranger)
91	    yazi                    # yazi shell (https://github.com/sxyazi/yazi)
92	    nnn                     # nnn shell (https://github.com/jarun/nnn)
93	    lf                      # lf shell (https://github.com/gokcehan/lf)
94	    xplr                    # xplr shell (https://github.com/sayanarijit/xplr)
95	    vim_shell               # vim shell indicator (:sh)
96	    midnight_commander      # midnight commander shell (https://midnight-commander.org/)
97	    nix_shell               # nix shell (https://nixos.org/nixos/nix-pills/developing-with-nix-shell.html)
98	    chezmoi_shell           # chezmoi shell (https://www.chezmoi.io/)
99	    # vpn_ip                # virtual private network indicator
100	    # load                  # CPU load
101	    # disk_usage            # disk usage
102	    # ram                   # free RAM
103	    # swap                  # used swap
104	    todo                    # todo items (https://github.com/todotxt/todo.txt-cli)
105	    timewarrior             # timewarrior tracking status (https://timewarrior.net/)
106	    taskwarrior             # taskwarrior task count (https://taskwarrior.org/)
107	    per_directory_history   # Oh My Zsh per-directory-history local/global indicator
108	    # cpu_arch              # CPU architecture
109	    # time                  # current time
110	    # =========================[ Line #2 ]=========================
111	    newline
112	    # ip                    # ip address and bandwidth usage for a specified network interface
113	    # public_ip             # public IP address
114	    # proxy                 # system-wide http/https/ftp proxy
115	    # battery               # internal battery
116	    # wifi                  # wifi speed
117	    # example               # example user-defined segment (see prompt_example function below)
118	  )
119	
120	  # Defines character set used by powerlevel10k. It's best to let `p10k configure` set it for you.
121	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_MODE=nerdfont-v3
122	  # When set to `moderate`, some icons will have an extra space after them. This is meant to avoid
123	  # icon overlap when using non-monospace fonts. When set to `none`, spaces are not added.
124	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ICON_PADDING=none
125	
126	  # Basic style options that define the overall look of your prompt. You probably don't want to
127	  # change them.
128	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_BACKGROUND=                            # transparent background
129	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_{LEFT,RIGHT}_{LEFT,RIGHT}_WHITESPACE=  # no surrounding whitespace
130	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_{LEFT,RIGHT}_SUBSEGMENT_SEPARATOR=' '  # separate segments with a space
131	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_{LEFT,RIGHT}_SEGMENT_SEPARATOR=        # no end-of-line symbol
132	
133	  # When set to true, icons appear before content on both sides of the prompt. When set
134	  # to false, icons go after content. If empty or not set, icons go before content in the left
135	  # prompt and after content in the right prompt.
136	  #
137	  # You can also override it for a specific segment:
138	  #
139	  #   POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_ICON_BEFORE_CONTENT=false
140	  #
141	  # Or for a specific segment in specific state:
142	  #
143	  #   POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_NOT_WRITABLE_ICON_BEFORE_CONTENT=false
144	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ICON_BEFORE_CONTENT=true
145	
146	  # Add an empty line before each prompt.
147	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PROMPT_ADD_NEWLINE=true
148	
149	  # Connect left prompt lines with these symbols.
150	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_MULTILINE_FIRST_PROMPT_PREFIX='%238F╭─'
151	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_MULTILINE_NEWLINE_PROMPT_PREFIX='%238F├─'
152	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_MULTILINE_LAST_PROMPT_PREFIX='%238F╰─'
153	  # Connect right prompt lines with these symbols.
154	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_MULTILINE_FIRST_PROMPT_SUFFIX='%238F─╮'
155	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_MULTILINE_NEWLINE_PROMPT_SUFFIX='%238F─┤'
156	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_MULTILINE_LAST_PROMPT_SUFFIX='%238F─╯'
157	
158	  # The left end of left prompt.
159	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_LEFT_PROMPT_FIRST_SEGMENT_START_SYMBOL=' '
160	  # The right end of right prompt.
161	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_RIGHT_PROMPT_LAST_SEGMENT_END_SYMBOL=' '
162	
163	  # Ruler, a.k.a. the horizontal line before each prompt. If you set it to true, you'll
164	  # probably want to set POWERLEVEL9K_PROMPT_ADD_NEWLINE=false above and
165	  # POWERLEVEL9K_MULTILINE_FIRST_PROMPT_GAP_CHAR=' ' below.
166	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_SHOW_RULER=false
167	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_RULER_CHAR='─'        # reasonable alternative: '·'
168	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_RULER_FOREGROUND=238
169	
170	  # Filler between left and right prompt on the first prompt line. You can set it to '·' or '─'
171	  # to make it easier to see the alignment between left and right prompt and to separate prompt
172	  # from command output. It serves the same purpose as ruler (see above) without increasing
173	  # the number of prompt lines. You'll probably want to set POWERLEVEL9K_SHOW_RULER=false
174	  # if using this. You might also like POWERLEVEL9K_PROMPT_ADD_NEWLINE=false for more compact
175	  # prompt.
176	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_MULTILINE_FIRST_PROMPT_GAP_CHAR='·'
177	  if [[ $POWERLEVEL9K_MULTILINE_FIRST_PROMPT_GAP_CHAR != ' ' ]]; then
178	    # The color of the filler.
179	    typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_MULTILINE_FIRST_PROMPT_GAP_FOREGROUND=238
180	    # Add a space between the end of left prompt and the filler.
181	    typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_LEFT_PROMPT_LAST_SEGMENT_END_SYMBOL=' '
182	    # Add a space between the filler and the start of right prompt.
183	    typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_RIGHT_PROMPT_FIRST_SEGMENT_START_SYMBOL=' '
184	    # Start filler from the edge of the screen if there are no left segments on the first line.
185	    typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_EMPTY_LINE_LEFT_PROMPT_FIRST_SEGMENT_END_SYMBOL='%{%}'
186	    # End filler on the edge of the screen if there are no right segments on the first line.
187	    typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_EMPTY_LINE_RIGHT_PROMPT_FIRST_SEGMENT_START_SYMBOL='%{%}'
188	  fi
189	
190	  #################################[ os_icon: os identifier ]##################################
191	  # OS identifier color.
192	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_OS_ICON_FOREGROUND=
193	  # Custom icon.
194	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_OS_ICON_CONTENT_EXPANSION='⭐'
195	
196	  ################################[ prompt_char: prompt symbol ]################################
197	  # Green prompt symbol if the last command succeeded.
198	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PROMPT_CHAR_OK_{VIINS,VICMD,VIVIS,VIOWR}_FOREGROUND=76
199	  # Red prompt symbol if the last command failed.
200	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PROMPT_CHAR_ERROR_{VIINS,VICMD,VIVIS,VIOWR}_FOREGROUND=196
201	  # Default prompt symbol.
202	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PROMPT_CHAR_{OK,ERROR}_VIINS_CONTENT_EXPANSION='❯'
203	  # Prompt symbol in command vi mode.
204	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PROMPT_CHAR_{OK,ERROR}_VICMD_CONTENT_EXPANSION='❮'
205	  # Prompt symbol in visual vi mode.
206	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PROMPT_CHAR_{OK,ERROR}_VIVIS_CONTENT_EXPANSION='V'
207	  # Prompt symbol in overwrite vi mode.
208	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PROMPT_CHAR_{OK,ERROR}_VIOWR_CONTENT_EXPANSION='▶'
209	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PROMPT_CHAR_OVERWRITE_STATE=true
210	  # No line terminator if prompt_char is the last segment.
211	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PROMPT_CHAR_LEFT_PROMPT_LAST_SEGMENT_END_SYMBOL=''
212	  # No line introducer if prompt_char is the first segment.
213	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PROMPT_CHAR_LEFT_PROMPT_FIRST_SEGMENT_START_SYMBOL=
214	
215	  ##################################[ dir: current directory ]##################################
216	  # Default current directory color.
217	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_FOREGROUND=31
218	  # If directory is too long, shorten some of its segments to the shortest possible unique
219	  # prefix. The shortened directory can be tab-completed to the original.
220	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_SHORTEN_STRATEGY=truncate_to_unique
221	  # Replace removed segment suffixes with this symbol.
222	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_SHORTEN_DELIMITER=
223	  # Color of the shortened directory segments.
224	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_SHORTENED_FOREGROUND=103
225	  # Color of the anchor directory segments. Anchor segments are never shortened. The first
226	  # segment is always an anchor.
227	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_ANCHOR_FOREGROUND=39
228	  # Display anchor directory segments in bold.
229	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_ANCHOR_BOLD=true
230	  # Don't shorten directories that contain any of these files. They are anchors.
231	  local anchor_files=(
232	    .bzr
233	    .citc
234	    .git
235	    .hg
236	    .node-version
237	    .python-version
238	    .go-version
239	    .ruby-version
240	    .lua-version
241	    .java-version
242	    .perl-version
243	    .php-version
244	    .tool-versions
245	    .mise.toml
246	    .shorten_folder_marker
247	    .svn
248	    .terraform
249	    CVS
250	    Cargo.toml
251	    composer.json
252	    go.mod
253	    package.json
254	    stack.yaml
255	  )
256	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_SHORTEN_FOLDER_MARKER="(${(j:|:)anchor_files})"
257	  # If set to "first" ("last"), remove everything before the first (last) subdirectory that contains
258	  # files matching $POWERLEVEL9K_SHORTEN_FOLDER_MARKER. For example, when the current directory is
259	  # /foo/bar/git_repo/nested_git_repo/baz, prompt will display git_repo/nested_git_repo/baz (first)
260	  # or nested_git_repo/baz (last). This assumes that git_repo and nested_git_repo contain markers
261	  # and other directories don't.
262	  #
263	  # Optionally, "first" and "last" can be followed by ":<offset>" where <offset> is an integer.
264	  # This moves the truncation point to the right (positive offset) or to the left (negative offset)
265	  # relative to the marker. Plain "first" and "last" are equivalent to "first:0" and "last:0"
266	  # respectively.
267	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_TRUNCATE_BEFORE_MARKER=false
268	  # Don't shorten this many last directory segments. They are anchors.
269	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_SHORTEN_DIR_LENGTH=1
270	  # Shorten directory if it's longer than this even if there is space for it. The value can
271	  # be either absolute (e.g., '80') or a percentage of terminal width (e.g, '50%'). If empty,
272	  # directory will be shortened only when prompt doesn't fit or when other parameters demand it
273	  # (see POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_MIN_COMMAND_COLUMNS and POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_MIN_COMMAND_COLUMNS_PCT below).
274	  # If set to `0`, directory will always be shortened to its minimum length.
275	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_MAX_LENGTH=80
276	  # When `dir` segment is on the last prompt line, try to shorten it enough to leave at least this
277	  # many columns for typing commands.
278	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_MIN_COMMAND_COLUMNS=40
279	  # When `dir` segment is on the last prompt line, try to shorten it enough to leave at least
280	  # COLUMNS * POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_MIN_COMMAND_COLUMNS_PCT * 0.01 columns for typing commands.
281	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_MIN_COMMAND_COLUMNS_PCT=50
282	  # If set to true, embed a hyperlink into the directory. Useful for quickly
283	  # opening a directory in the file manager simply by clicking the link.
284	  # Can also be handy when the directory is shortened, as it allows you to see
285	  # the full directory that was used in previous commands.
286	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_HYPERLINK=false
287	
288	  # Enable special styling for non-writable and non-existent directories. See POWERLEVEL9K_LOCK_ICON
289	  # and POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_CLASSES below.
290	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_SHOW_WRITABLE=v3
291	
292	  # The default icon shown next to non-writable and non-existent directories when
293	  # POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_SHOW_WRITABLE is set to v3.
294	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_LOCK_ICON='⭐'
295	
296	  # POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_CLASSES allows you to specify custom icons and colors for different
297	  # directories. It must be an array with 3 * N elements. Each triplet consists of:
298	  #
299	  #   1. A pattern against which the current directory ($PWD) is matched. Matching is done with
300	  #      extended_glob option enabled.
301	  #   2. Directory class for the purpose of styling.
302	  #   3. An empty string.
303	  #
304	  # Triplets are tried in order. The first triplet whose pattern matches $PWD wins.
305	  #
306	  # If POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_SHOW_WRITABLE is set to v3, non-writable and non-existent directories
307	  # acquire class suffix _NOT_WRITABLE and NON_EXISTENT respectively.
308	  #
309	  # For example, given these settings:
310	  #
311	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_CLASSES=(
312	  #     '~/work(|/*)'  WORK     ''
313	  #     '~(|/*)'       HOME     ''
314	  #     '*'            DEFAULT  '')
315	  #
316	  # Whenever the current directory is ~/work or a subdirectory of ~/work, it gets styled with one
317	  # of the following classes depending on its writability and existence: WORK, WORK_NOT_WRITABLE or
318	  # WORK_NON_EXISTENT.
319	  #
320	  # Simply assigning classes to directories doesn't have any visible effects. It merely gives you an
321	  # option to define custom colors and icons for different directory classes.
322	  #
323	  #   # Styling for WORK.
324	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
325	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_FOREGROUND=31
326	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_SHORTENED_FOREGROUND=103
327	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_ANCHOR_FOREGROUND=39
328	  #
329	  #   # Styling for WORK_NOT_WRITABLE.
330	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_NOT_WRITABLE_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
331	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_NOT_WRITABLE_FOREGROUND=31
332	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_NOT_WRITABLE_SHORTENED_FOREGROUND=103
333	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_NOT_WRITABLE_ANCHOR_FOREGROUND=39
334	  #
335	  #   # Styling for WORK_NON_EXISTENT.
336	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_NON_EXISTENT_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
337	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_NON_EXISTENT_FOREGROUND=31
338	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_NON_EXISTENT_SHORTENED_FOREGROUND=103
339	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_NON_EXISTENT_ANCHOR_FOREGROUND=39
340	  #
341	  # If a styling parameter isn't explicitly defined for some class, it falls back to the classless
342	  # parameter. For example, if POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_WORK_NOT_WRITABLE_FOREGROUND is not set, it falls
343	  # back to POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_FOREGROUND.
344	  #
345	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_CLASSES=()
346	
347	  # Custom prefix.
348	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIR_PREFIX='%fin '
349	
350	  #####################################[ vcs: git status ]######################################
351	  # Branch icon. Set this parameter to '\UE0A0 ' for the popular Powerline branch icon.
352	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_BRANCH_ICON='\uF126 '
353	
354	  # Untracked files icon. It's really a question mark, your font isn't broken.
355	  # Change the value of this parameter to show a different icon.
356	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_UNTRACKED_ICON='?'
357	
358	  # Formatter for Git status.
359	  #
360	  # Example output: master wip ⇣42⇡42 *42 merge ~42 +42 !42 ?42.
361	  #
362	  # You can edit the function to customize how Git status looks.
363	  #
364	  # VCS_STATUS_* parameters are set by gitstatus plugin. See reference:
365	  # https://github.com/romkatv/gitstatus/blob/master/gitstatus.plugin.zsh.
366	  function my_git_formatter() {
367	    emulate -L zsh
368	
369	    if [[ -n $P9K_CONTENT ]]; then
370	      # If P9K_CONTENT is not empty, use it. It's either "loading" or from vcs_info (not from
371	      # gitstatus plugin). VCS_STATUS_* parameters are not available in this case.
372	      typeset -g my_git_format=$P9K_CONTENT
373	      return
374	    fi
375	
376	    if (( $1 )); then
377	      # Styling for up-to-date Git status.
378	      local       meta='%f'     # default foreground
379	      local      clean='%76F'   # green foreground
380	      local   modified='%178F'  # yellow foreground
381	      local  untracked='%39F'   # blue foreground
382	      local conflicted='%196F'  # red foreground
383	    else
384	      # Styling for incomplete and stale Git status.
385	      local       meta='%244F'  # grey foreground
386	      local      clean='%244F'  # grey foreground
387	      local   modified='%244F'  # grey foreground
388	      local  untracked='%244F'  # grey foreground
389	      local conflicted='%244F'  # grey foreground
390	    fi
391	
392	    local res
393	
394	    if [[ -n $VCS_STATUS_LOCAL_BRANCH ]]; then
395	      local branch=${(V)VCS_STATUS_LOCAL_BRANCH}
396	      # If local branch name is at most 32 characters long, show it in full.
397	      # Otherwise show the first 12 … the last 12.
398	      # Tip: To always show local branch name in full without truncation, delete the next line.
399	      (( $#branch > 32 )) && branch[13,-13]="…"  # <-- this line
400	      res+="${clean}${(g::)POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_BRANCH_ICON}${branch//\%/%%}"
401	    fi
402	
403	    if [[ -n $VCS_STATUS_TAG
404	          # Show tag only if not on a branch.
405	          # Tip: To always show tag, delete the next line.
406	          && -z $VCS_STATUS_LOCAL_BRANCH  # <-- this line
407	        ]]; then
408	      local tag=${(V)VCS_STATUS_TAG}
409	      # If tag name is at most 32 characters long, show it in full.
410	      # Otherwise show the first 12 … the last 12.
411	      # Tip: To always show tag name in full without truncation, delete the next line.
412	      (( $#tag > 32 )) && tag[13,-13]="…"  # <-- this line
413	      res+="${meta}#${clean}${tag//\%/%%}"
414	    fi
415	
416	    # Display the current Git commit if there is no branch and no tag.
417	    # Tip: To always display the current Git commit, delete the next line.
418	    [[ -z $VCS_STATUS_LOCAL_BRANCH && -z $VCS_STATUS_TAG ]] &&  # <-- this line
419	      res+="${meta}@${clean}${VCS_STATUS_COMMIT[1,8]}"
420	
421	    # Show tracking branch name if it differs from local branch.
422	    if [[ -n ${VCS_STATUS_REMOTE_BRANCH:#$VCS_STATUS_LOCAL_BRANCH} ]]; then
423	      res+="${meta}:${clean}${(V)VCS_STATUS_REMOTE_BRANCH//\%/%%}"
424	    fi
425	
426	    # Display "wip" if the latest commit's summary contains "wip" or "WIP".
427	    if [[ $VCS_STATUS_COMMIT_SUMMARY == (|*[^[:alnum:]])(wip|WIP)(|[^[:alnum:]]*) ]]; then
428	      res+=" ${modified}wip"
429	    fi
430	
431	    if (( VCS_STATUS_COMMITS_AHEAD || VCS_STATUS_COMMITS_BEHIND )); then
432	      # ⇣42 if behind the remote.
433	      (( VCS_STATUS_COMMITS_BEHIND )) && res+=" ${clean}⇣${VCS_STATUS_COMMITS_BEHIND}"
434	      # ⇡42 if ahead of the remote; no leading space if also behind the remote: ⇣42⇡42.
435	      (( VCS_STATUS_COMMITS_AHEAD && !VCS_STATUS_COMMITS_BEHIND )) && res+=" "
436	      (( VCS_STATUS_COMMITS_AHEAD  )) && res+="${clean}⇡${VCS_STATUS_COMMITS_AHEAD}"
437	    elif [[ -n $VCS_STATUS_REMOTE_BRANCH ]]; then
438	      # Tip: Uncomment the next line to display '=' if up to date with the remote.
439	      # res+=" ${clean}="
440	    fi
441	
442	    # ⇠42 if behind the push remote.
443	    (( VCS_STATUS_PUSH_COMMITS_BEHIND )) && res+=" ${clean}⇠${VCS_STATUS_PUSH_COMMITS_BEHIND}"
444	    (( VCS_STATUS_PUSH_COMMITS_AHEAD && !VCS_STATUS_PUSH_COMMITS_BEHIND )) && res+=" "
445	    # ⇢42 if ahead of the push remote; no leading space if also behind: ⇠42⇢42.
446	    (( VCS_STATUS_PUSH_COMMITS_AHEAD  )) && res+="${clean}⇢${VCS_STATUS_PUSH_COMMITS_AHEAD}"
447	    # *42 if have stashes.
448	    (( VCS_STATUS_STASHES        )) && res+=" ${clean}*${VCS_STATUS_STASHES}"
449	    # 'merge' if the repo is in an unusual state.
450	    [[ -n $VCS_STATUS_ACTION     ]] && res+=" ${conflicted}${VCS_STATUS_ACTION}"
451	    # ~42 if have merge conflicts.
452	    (( VCS_STATUS_NUM_CONFLICTED )) && res+=" ${conflicted}~${VCS_STATUS_NUM_CONFLICTED}"
453	    # +42 if have staged changes.
454	    (( VCS_STATUS_NUM_STAGED     )) && res+=" ${modified}+${VCS_STATUS_NUM_STAGED}"
455	    # !42 if have unstaged changes.
456	    (( VCS_STATUS_NUM_UNSTAGED   )) && res+=" ${modified}!${VCS_STATUS_NUM_UNSTAGED}"
457	    # ?42 if have untracked files. It's really a question mark, your font isn't broken.
458	    # See POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_UNTRACKED_ICON above if you want to use a different icon.
459	    # Remove the next line if you don't want to see untracked files at all.
460	    (( VCS_STATUS_NUM_UNTRACKED  )) && res+=" ${untracked}${(g::)POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_UNTRACKED_ICON}${VCS_STATUS_NUM_UNTRACKED}"
461	    # "─" if the number of unstaged files is unknown. This can happen due to
462	    # POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_MAX_INDEX_SIZE_DIRTY (see below) being set to a non-negative number lower
463	    # than the number of files in the Git index, or due to bash.showDirtyState being set to false
464	    # in the repository config. The number of staged and untracked files may also be unknown
465	    # in this case.
466	    (( VCS_STATUS_HAS_UNSTAGED == -1 )) && res+=" ${modified}─"
467	
468	    typeset -g my_git_format=$res
469	  }
470	  functions -M my_git_formatter 2>/dev/null
471	
472	  # Don't count the number of unstaged, untracked and conflicted files in Git repositories with
473	  # more than this many files in the index. Negative value means infinity.
474	  #
475	  # If you are working in Git repositories with tens of millions of files and seeing performance
476	  # sagging, try setting POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_MAX_INDEX_SIZE_DIRTY to a number lower than the output
477	  # of `git ls-files | wc -l`. Alternatively, add `bash.showDirtyState = false` to the repository's
478	  # config: `git config bash.showDirtyState false`.
479	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_MAX_INDEX_SIZE_DIRTY=-1
480	
481	  # Don't show Git status in prompt for repositories whose workdir matches this pattern.
482	  # For example, if set to '~', the Git repository at $HOME/.git will be ignored.
483	  # Multiple patterns can be combined with '|': '~(|/foo)|/bar/baz/*'.
484	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_DISABLED_WORKDIR_PATTERN='~'
485	
486	  # Disable the default Git status formatting.
487	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_DISABLE_GITSTATUS_FORMATTING=true
488	  # Install our own Git status formatter.
489	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_CONTENT_EXPANSION='${$((my_git_formatter(1)))+${my_git_format}}'
490	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_LOADING_CONTENT_EXPANSION='${$((my_git_formatter(0)))+${my_git_format}}'
491	  # Enable counters for staged, unstaged, etc.
492	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_{STAGED,UNSTAGED,UNTRACKED,CONFLICTED,COMMITS_AHEAD,COMMITS_BEHIND}_MAX_NUM=-1
493	
494	  # Icon color.
495	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_COLOR=76
496	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_LOADING_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_COLOR=244
497	  # Custom icon.
498	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
499	  # Custom prefix.
500	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_PREFIX='%fon '
501	
502	  # Show status of repositories of these types. You can add svn and/or hg if you are
503	  # using them. If you do, your prompt may become slow even when your current directory
504	  # isn't in an svn or hg repository.
505	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_BACKENDS=(git)
506	
507	  # These settings are used for repositories other than Git or when gitstatusd fails and
508	  # Powerlevel10k has to fall back to using vcs_info.
509	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_CLEAN_FOREGROUND=76
510	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_UNTRACKED_FOREGROUND=76
511	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VCS_MODIFIED_FOREGROUND=178
512	
513	  ##########################[ status: exit code of the last command ]###########################
514	  # Enable OK_PIPE, ERROR_PIPE and ERROR_SIGNAL status states to allow us to enable, disable and
515	  # style them independently from the regular OK and ERROR state.
516	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_EXTENDED_STATES=true
517	
518	  # Status on success. No content, just an icon. No need to show it if prompt_char is enabled as
519	  # it will signify success by turning green.
520	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_OK=false
521	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_OK_FOREGROUND=70
522	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_OK_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='✔'
523	
524	  # Status when some part of a pipe command fails but the overall exit status is zero. It may look
525	  # like this: 1|0.
526	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_OK_PIPE=true
527	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_OK_PIPE_FOREGROUND=70
528	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_OK_PIPE_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='✔'
529	
530	  # Status when it's just an error code (e.g., '1'). No need to show it if prompt_char is enabled as
531	  # it will signify error by turning red.
532	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_ERROR=false
533	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_ERROR_FOREGROUND=160
534	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_ERROR_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='✘'
535	
536	  # Status when the last command was terminated by a signal.
537	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_ERROR_SIGNAL=true
538	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_ERROR_SIGNAL_FOREGROUND=160
539	  # Use terse signal names: "INT" instead of "SIGINT(2)".
540	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_VERBOSE_SIGNAME=false
541	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_ERROR_SIGNAL_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='✘'
542	
543	  # Status when some part of a pipe command fails and the overall exit status is also non-zero.
544	  # It may look like this: 1|0.
545	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_ERROR_PIPE=true
546	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_ERROR_PIPE_FOREGROUND=160
547	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_STATUS_ERROR_PIPE_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='✘'
548	
549	  ###################[ command_execution_time: duration of the last command ]###################
550	  # Show duration of the last command if takes at least this many seconds.
551	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_COMMAND_EXECUTION_TIME_THRESHOLD=3
552	  # Show this many fractional digits. Zero means round to seconds.
553	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_COMMAND_EXECUTION_TIME_PRECISION=0
554	  # Execution time color.
555	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_COMMAND_EXECUTION_TIME_FOREGROUND=101
556	  # Duration format: 1d 2h 3m 4s.
557	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_COMMAND_EXECUTION_TIME_FORMAT='d h m s'
558	  # Custom icon.
559	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_COMMAND_EXECUTION_TIME_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
560	  # Custom prefix.
561	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_COMMAND_EXECUTION_TIME_PREFIX='%ftook '
562	
563	  #######################[ background_jobs: presence of background jobs ]#######################
564	  # Don't show the number of background jobs.
565	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_BACKGROUND_JOBS_VERBOSE=false
566	  # Background jobs color.
567	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_BACKGROUND_JOBS_FOREGROUND=70
568	  # Custom icon.
569	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_BACKGROUND_JOBS_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
570	
571	  #######################[ direnv: direnv status (https://direnv.net/) ]########################
572	  # Direnv color.
573	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIRENV_FOREGROUND=178
574	  # Custom icon.
575	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DIRENV_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
576	
577	  ###############[ asdf: asdf version manager (https://github.com/asdf-vm/asdf) ]###############
578	  # Default asdf color. Only used to display tools for which there is no color override (see below).
579	  # Tip:  Override this parameter for ${TOOL} with POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_${TOOL}_FOREGROUND.
580	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_FOREGROUND=66
581	
582	  # There are four parameters that can be used to hide asdf tools. Each parameter describes
583	  # conditions under which a tool gets hidden. Parameters can hide tools but not unhide them. If at
584	  # least one parameter decides to hide a tool, that tool gets hidden. If no parameter decides to
585	  # hide a tool, it gets shown.
586	  #
587	  # Special note on the difference between POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_SOURCES and
588	  # POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_PROMPT_ALWAYS_SHOW. Consider the effect of the following commands:
589	  #
590	  #   asdf local  python 3.8.1
591	  #   asdf global python 3.8.1
592	  #
593	  # After running both commands the current python version is 3.8.1 and its source is "local" as
594	  # it takes precedence over "global". If POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_PROMPT_ALWAYS_SHOW is set to false,
595	  # it'll hide python version in this case because 3.8.1 is the same as the global version.
596	  # POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_SOURCES will hide python version only if the value of this parameter doesn't
597	  # contain "local".
598	
599	  # Hide tool versions that don't come from one of these sources.
600	  #
601	  # Available sources:
602	  #
603	  # - shell   `asdf current` says "set by ASDF_${TOOL}_VERSION environment variable"
604	  # - local   `asdf current` says "set by /some/not/home/directory/file"
605	  # - global  `asdf current` says "set by /home/username/file"
606	  #
607	  # Note: If this parameter is set to (shell local global), it won't hide tools.
608	  # Tip:  Override this parameter for ${TOOL} with POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_${TOOL}_SOURCES.
609	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_SOURCES=(shell local global)
610	
611	  # If set to false, hide tool versions that are the same as global.
612	  #
613	  # Note: The name of this parameter doesn't reflect its meaning at all.
614	  # Note: If this parameter is set to true, it won't hide tools.
615	  # Tip:  Override this parameter for ${TOOL} with POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_${TOOL}_PROMPT_ALWAYS_SHOW.
616	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_PROMPT_ALWAYS_SHOW=false
617	
618	  # If set to false, hide tool versions that are equal to "system".
619	  #
620	  # Note: If this parameter is set to true, it won't hide tools.
621	  # Tip: Override this parameter for ${TOOL} with POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_${TOOL}_SHOW_SYSTEM.
622	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_SHOW_SYSTEM=true
623	
624	  # If set to non-empty value, hide tools unless there is a file matching the specified file pattern
625	  # in the current directory, or its parent directory, or its grandparent directory, and so on.
626	  #
627	  # Note: If this parameter is set to empty value, it won't hide tools.
628	  # Note: SHOW_ON_UPGLOB isn't specific to asdf. It works with all prompt segments.
629	  # Tip: Override this parameter for ${TOOL} with POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_${TOOL}_SHOW_ON_UPGLOB.
630	  #
631	  # Example: Hide nodejs version when there is no package.json and no *.js files in the current
632	  # directory, in `..`, in `../..` and so on.
633	  #
634	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_NODEJS_SHOW_ON_UPGLOB='*.js|package.json'
635	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_SHOW_ON_UPGLOB=
636	
637	  # Ruby version from asdf.
638	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_RUBY_FOREGROUND=168
639	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_RUBY_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
640	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ASDF_RUBY_SHOW_ON_UPGLOB='*.foo|*.bar'

812	  ######################################[ load: CPU load ]######################################

815	  # Load color when load is under 50%.
816	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_LOAD_NORMAL_FOREGROUND=66
817	  # Load color when load is between 50% and 70%.
818	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_LOAD_WARNING_FOREGROUND=178
819	  # Load color when load is over 70%.
820	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_LOAD_CRITICAL_FOREGROUND=166

823	
824	  ################[ todo: todo items (https://github.com/todotxt/todo.txt-cli) ]################
825	  # Todo color.
826	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TODO_FOREGROUND=110
827	  # Hide todo when the total number of tasks is zero.
828	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TODO_HIDE_ZERO_TOTAL=true
829	  # Hide todo when the number of tasks after filtering is zero.
830	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TODO_HIDE_ZERO_FILTERED=false
831	
832	  # Todo format. The following parameters are available within the expansion.
833	  #
834	  # - P9K_TODO_TOTAL_TASK_COUNT     The total number of tasks.
835	  # - P9K_TODO_FILTERED_TASK_COUNT  The number of tasks after filtering.
836	  #
837	  # These variables correspond to the last line of the output of `todo.sh -p ls`:
838	  #
839	  #   TODO: 24 of 42 tasks shown
840	  #
841	  # Here 24 is P9K_TODO_FILTERED_TASK_COUNT and 42 is P9K_TODO_TOTAL_TASK_COUNT.
842	  #
843	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TODO_CONTENT_EXPANSION='$P9K_TODO_FILTERED_TASK_COUNT'
844	
845	  # Custom icon.
846	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TODO_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
847	
848	  ###########[ timewarrior: timewarrior tracking status (https://timewarrior.net/) ]############
849	  # Timewarrior color.
850	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TIMEWARRIOR_FOREGROUND=110
851	  # If the tracked task is longer than 24 characters, truncate and append "…".
852	  # Tip: To always display tasks without truncation, delete the following parameter.
853	  # Tip: To hide task names and display just the icon when time tracking is enabled, set the
854	  # value of the following parameter to "".
855	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TIMEWARRIOR_CONTENT_EXPANSION='${P9K_CONTENT:0:24}${${P9K_CONTENT:24}:+…}'
856	
857	  # Custom icon.
858	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TIMEWARRIOR_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
859	
860	  ##############[ taskwarrior: taskwarrior task count (https://taskwarrior.org/) ]##############
861	  # Taskwarrior color.
862	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TASKWARRIOR_FOREGROUND=74
863	
864	  # Taskwarrior segment format. The following parameters are available within the expansion.
865	  #
866	  # - P9K_TASKWARRIOR_PENDING_COUNT   The number of pending tasks: `task +PENDING count`.
867	  # - P9K_TASKWARRIOR_OVERDUE_COUNT   The number of overdue tasks: `task +OVERDUE count`.
868	  #
869	  # Zero values are represented as empty parameters.
870	  #
871	  # The default format:
872	  #
873	  #   '${P9K_TASKWARRIOR_OVERDUE_COUNT:+"!$P9K_TASKWARRIOR_OVERDUE_COUNT/"}$P9K_TASKWARRIOR_PENDING_COUNT'
874	  #
875	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TASKWARRIOR_CONTENT_EXPANSION='$P9K_TASKWARRIOR_PENDING_COUNT'
876	
877	  # Custom icon.
878	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TASKWARRIOR_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'

885	  # Tip: Uncomment the next two lines to hide "local"/"global" text and leave just the icon.

904	  ##################################[ context: user@hostname ]##################################
905	  # Context color when running with privileges.
906	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_CONTEXT_ROOT_FOREGROUND=178
907	  # Context color in SSH without privileges.
908	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_CONTEXT_{REMOTE,REMOTE_SUDO}_FOREGROUND=180
909	  # Default context color (no privileges, no SSH).
910	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_CONTEXT_FOREGROUND=180
911	
912	  # Context format when running with privileges: bold user@hostname.
913	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_CONTEXT_ROOT_TEMPLATE='%B%n@%m'
914	  # Context format when in SSH without privileges: user@hostname.
915	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_CONTEXT_{REMOTE,REMOTE_SUDO}_TEMPLATE='%n@%m'
916	  # Default context format (no privileges, no SSH): user@hostname.
917	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_CONTEXT_TEMPLATE='%n@%m'
918	
919	  # Don't show context unless running with privileges or in SSH.
920	  # Tip: Remove the next line to always show context.
921	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_CONTEXT_{DEFAULT,SUDO}_{CONTENT,VISUAL_IDENTIFIER}_EXPANSION=
922	
923	  # Custom icon.
924	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_CONTEXT_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
925	  # Custom prefix.
926	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_CONTEXT_PREFIX='%fwith '
927	
928	  ###[ virtualenv: python virtual environment (https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html) ]###
929	  # Python virtual environment color.
930	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VIRTUALENV_FOREGROUND=37
931	  # Don't show Python version next to the virtual environment name.
932	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VIRTUALENV_SHOW_PYTHON_VERSION=false
933	  # If set to "false", won't show virtualenv if pyenv is already shown.
934	  # If set to "if-different", won't show virtualenv if it's the same as pyenv.
935	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VIRTUALENV_SHOW_WITH_PYENV=false
936	  # Separate environment name from Python version only with a space.
937	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VIRTUALENV_{LEFT,RIGHT}_DELIMITER=
938	  # Custom icon.
939	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VIRTUALENV_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
940	
941	  #####################[ anaconda: conda environment (https://conda.io/) ]######################
942	  # Anaconda environment color.
943	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ANACONDA_FOREGROUND=37
944	
945	  # Anaconda segment format. The following parameters are available within the expansion.
946	  #
947	  # - CONDA_PREFIX                 Absolute path to the active Anaconda/Miniconda environment.
948	  # - CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV            Name of the active Anaconda/Miniconda environment.
949	  # - CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER        Configurable prompt modifier (see below).
950	  # - P9K_ANACONDA_PYTHON_VERSION  Current python version (python --version).
951	  #
952	  # CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER can be configured with the following command:
953	  #
954	  #   conda config --set env_prompt '({default_env}) '
955	  #
956	  # The last argument is a Python format string that can use the following variables:
957	  #
958	  # - prefix       The same as CONDA_PREFIX.
959	  # - default_env  The same as CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV.
960	  # - name         The last segment of CONDA_PREFIX.
961	  # - stacked_env  Comma-separated list of names in the environment stack. The first element is
962	  #                always the same as default_env.
963	  #
964	  # Note: '({default_env}) ' is the default value of env_prompt.
965	  #
966	  # The default value of POWERLEVEL9K_ANACONDA_CONTENT_EXPANSION expands to $CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER
967	  # without the surrounding parentheses, or to the last path component of CONDA_PREFIX if the former
968	  # is empty.
969	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ANACONDA_CONTENT_EXPANSION='${${${${CONDA_PROMPT_MODIFIER#\(}% }%\)}:-${CONDA_PREFIX:t}}'
970	
971	  # Custom icon.
972	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_ANACONDA_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
973	
974	  ################[ pyenv: python environment (https://github.com/pyenv/pyenv) ]################
975	  # Pyenv color.
976	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PYENV_FOREGROUND=37
977	  # Hide python version if it doesn't come from one of these sources.
978	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PYENV_SOURCES=(shell local global)
979	  # If set to false, hide python version if it's the same as global:
980	  # $(pyenv version-name) == $(pyenv global).
981	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PYENV_PROMPT_ALWAYS_SHOW=false
982	  # If set to false, hide python version if it's equal to "system".
983	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PYENV_SHOW_SYSTEM=true
984	
985	  # Pyenv segment format. The following parameters are available within the expansion.
986	  #
987	  # - P9K_CONTENT                Current pyenv environment (pyenv version-name).
988	  # - P9K_PYENV_PYTHON_VERSION   Current python version (python --version).
989	  #
990	  # The default format has the following logic:
991	  #
992	  # 1. Display just "$P9K_CONTENT" if it's equal to "$P9K_PYENV_PYTHON_VERSION" or
993	  #    starts with "$P9K_PYENV_PYTHON_VERSION/".
994	  # 2. Otherwise display "$P9K_CONTENT $P9K_PYENV_PYTHON_VERSION".
995	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PYENV_CONTENT_EXPANSION='${P9K_CONTENT}${${P9K_CONTENT:#$P9K_PYENV_PYTHON_VERSION(|/*)}:+ $P9K_PYENV_PYTHON_VERSION}'
996	
997	  # Custom icon.
998	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PYENV_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'

1204	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PHPENV_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1205	
1206	  #######[ scalaenv: scala version from scalaenv (https://github.com/scalaenv/scalaenv) ]#######
1207	  # Scala color.
1208	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_SCALAENV_FOREGROUND=160
1209	  # Hide scala version if it doesn't come from one of these sources.

1211	  # If set to false, hide scala version if it's the same as global:
1212	  # $(scalaenv version-name) == $(scalaenv global).
1213	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_SCALAENV_PROMPT_ALWAYS_SHOW=false
1214	  # If set to false, hide scala version if it's equal to "system".
1215	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_SCALAENV_SHOW_SYSTEM=true
1216	  # Custom icon.
1217	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_SCALAENV_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1218	
1219	  ##########[ haskell_stack: haskell version from stack (https://haskellstack.org/) ]###########
1220	  # Haskell color.
1221	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_HASKELL_STACK_FOREGROUND=172
1222	  # Hide haskell version if it doesn't come from one of these sources.
1223	  #
1224	  #   shell:  version is set by STACK_YAML
1225	  #   local:  version is set by stack.yaml up the directory tree
1226	  #   global: version is set by the implicit global project (~/.stack/global-project/stack.yaml)
1227	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_HASKELL_STACK_SOURCES=(shell local)
1228	  # If set to false, hide haskell version if it's the same as in the implicit global project.
1229	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_HASKELL_STACK_ALWAYS_SHOW=true
1230	  # Custom icon.
1231	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_HASKELL_STACK_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'

1233	  #############[ kubecontext: current kubernetes context (https://kubernetes.io/) ]#############
1234	  # Show kubecontext only when the command you are typing invokes one of these tools.
1235	  # Tip: Remove the next line to always show kubecontext.
1236	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_SHOW_ON_COMMAND='kubectl|helm|kubens|kubectx|oc|istioctl|kogito|k9s|helmfile|flux|fluxctl|stern|kubeseal|skaffold|kubent|kubecolor|cmctl|sparkctl'
1237	
1238	  # Kubernetes context classes for the purpose of using different colors, icons and expansions with
1239	  # different contexts.
1240	  #
1241	  # POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLASSES is an array with even number of elements. The first element
1242	  # in each pair defines a pattern against which the current kubernetes context gets matched.
1243	  # More specifically, it's P9K_CONTENT prior to the application of context expansion (see below)
1244	  # that gets matched. If you unset all POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_*CONTENT_EXPANSION parameters,
1245	  # you'll see this value in your prompt. The second element of each pair in
1246	  # POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLASSES defines the context class. Patterns are tried in order. The
1247	  # first match wins.
1248	  #
1249	  # For example, given these settings:
1250	  #
1251	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLASSES=(
1252	  #     '*prod*'  PROD
1253	  #     '*test*'  TEST
1254	  #     '*'       DEFAULT)
1255	  #
1256	  # If your current kubernetes context is "deathray-testing/default", its class is TEST
1257	  # because "deathray-testing/default" doesn't match the pattern '*prod*' but does match '*test*'.
1258	  #
1259	  # You can define different colors, icons and content expansions for different classes:
1260	  #
1261	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_TEST_FOREGROUND=28
1262	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_TEST_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1263	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_TEST_CONTENT_EXPANSION='> ${P9K_CONTENT} <'
1264	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLASSES=(
1265	      # '*prod*'  PROD    # These values are examples that are unlikely
1266	      # '*test*'  TEST    # to match your needs. Customize them as needed.
1267	      '*'       DEFAULT)
1268	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_DEFAULT_FOREGROUND=134
1269	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_DEFAULT_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1270	
1271	  # Use POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_CONTENT_EXPANSION to specify the content displayed by kubecontext
1272	  # segment. Parameter expansions are very flexible and fast, too. See reference:
1273	  # http://zsh.sourceforge.net/Doc/Release/Expansion.html#Parameter-Expansion.
1274	  #
1275	  # Within the expansion the following parameters are always available:
1276	  #
1277	  # - P9K_CONTENT                The content that would've been displayed if there was no content
1278	  #                              expansion defined.
1279	  # - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_NAME       The current context's name. Corresponds to column NAME in the
1280	  #                              output of `kubectl config get-contexts`.
1281	  # - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLUSTER    The current context's cluster. Corresponds to column CLUSTER in the
1282	  #                              output of `kubectl config get-contexts`.
1283	  # - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_NAMESPACE  The current context's namespace. Corresponds to column NAMESPACE
1284	  #                              in the output of `kubectl config get-contexts`. If there is no
1285	  #                              namespace, the parameter is set to "default".
1286	  # - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_USER       The current context's user. Corresponds to column AUTHINFO in the
1287	  #                              output of `kubectl config get-contexts`.
1288	  #
1289	  # If the context points to Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) or Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS),
1290	  # the following extra parameters are available:
1291	  #
1292	  # - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_NAME     Either "gke" or "eks".
1293	  # - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_ACCOUNT  Account/project ID.
1294	  # - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_ZONE     Availability zone.
1295	  # - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_CLUSTER  Cluster.
1296	  #
1297	  # P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_* parameters are derived from P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLUSTER. For example,
1298	  # if P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLUSTER is "gke_my-account_us-east1-a_my-cluster-01":
1299	  #
1300	  #   - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_NAME=gke
1301	  #   - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_ACCOUNT=my-account
1302	  #   - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_ZONE=us-east1-a
1303	  #   - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_CLUSTER=my-cluster-01
1304	  #
1305	  # If P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLUSTER is "arn:aws:eks:us-east-1:123456789012:cluster/my-cluster-01":
1306	  #
1307	  #   - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_NAME=eks
1308	  #   - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_ACCOUNT=123456789012
1309	  #   - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_ZONE=us-east-1
1310	  #   - P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_CLUSTER=my-cluster-01
1311	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_DEFAULT_CONTENT_EXPANSION=
1312	  # Show P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_CLUSTER if it's not empty and fall back to P9K_KUBECONTEXT_NAME.
1313	  POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_DEFAULT_CONTENT_EXPANSION+='${P9K_KUBECONTEXT_CLOUD_CLUSTER:-${P9K_KUBECONTEXT_NAME}}'
1314	  # Append the current context's namespace if it's not "default".
1315	  POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_DEFAULT_CONTENT_EXPANSION+='${${:-/$P9K_KUBECONTEXT_NAMESPACE}:#/default}'
1316	
1317	  # Custom prefix.
1318	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_KUBECONTEXT_PREFIX='%fat '
1319	
1320	  ################[ terraform: terraform workspace (https://www.terraform.io) ]#################
1321	  # Don't show terraform workspace if it's literally "default".
1322	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_SHOW_DEFAULT=false
1323	  # POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_CLASSES is an array with even number of elements. The first element
1324	  # in each pair defines a pattern against which the current terraform workspace gets matched.
1325	  # More specifically, it's P9K_CONTENT prior to the application of context expansion (see below)
1326	  # that gets matched. If you unset all POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_*CONTENT_EXPANSION parameters,
1327	  # you'll see this value in your prompt. The second element of each pair in
1328	  # POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_CLASSES defines the workspace class. Patterns are tried in order. The
1329	  # first match wins.
1330	  #
1331	  # For example, given these settings:
1332	  #
1333	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_CLASSES=(
1334	  #     '*prod*'  PROD
1335	  #     '*test*'  TEST
1336	  #     '*'       OTHER)
1337	  #
1338	  # If your current terraform workspace is "project_test", its class is TEST because "project_test"
1339	  # doesn't match the pattern '*prod*' but does match '*test*'.
1340	  #
1341	  # You can define different colors, icons and content expansions for different classes:
1342	  #
1343	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_TEST_FOREGROUND=28
1344	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_TEST_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1345	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_TEST_CONTENT_EXPANSION='> ${P9K_CONTENT} <'
1346	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_CLASSES=(
1347	      # '*prod*'  PROD    # These values are examples that are unlikely
1348	      # '*test*'  TEST    # to match your needs. Customize them as needed.
1349	      '*'         OTHER)
1350	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_OTHER_FOREGROUND=38
1351	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_OTHER_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1352	
1353	  #############[ terraform_version: terraform version (https://www.terraform.io) ]##############
1354	  # Terraform version color.
1355	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_VERSION_FOREGROUND=38
1356	  # Custom icon.
1357	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TERRAFORM_VERSION_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1358	
1359	  #[ aws: aws profile (https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/userguide/cli-configure-profiles.html) ]#
1360	  # Show aws only when the command you are typing invokes one of these tools.
1361	  # Tip: Remove the next line to always show aws.
1362	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_SHOW_ON_COMMAND='aws|awless|cdk|terraform|pulumi|terragrunt'
1363	
1364	  # POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_CLASSES is an array with even number of elements. The first element
1365	  # in each pair defines a pattern against which the current AWS profile gets matched.
1366	  # More specifically, it's P9K_CONTENT prior to the application of context expansion (see below)
1367	  # that gets matched. If you unset all POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_*CONTENT_EXPANSION parameters,
1368	  # you'll see this value in your prompt. The second element of each pair in
1369	  # POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_CLASSES defines the profile class. Patterns are tried in order. The
1370	  # first match wins.
1371	  #
1372	  # For example, given these settings:
1373	  #
1374	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_CLASSES=(
1375	  #     '*prod*'  PROD
1376	  #     '*test*'  TEST
1377	  #     '*'       DEFAULT)
1378	  #
1379	  # If your current AWS profile is "company_test", its class is TEST
1380	  # because "company_test" doesn't match the pattern '*prod*' but does match '*test*'.
1381	  #
1382	  # You can define different colors, icons and content expansions for different classes:
1383	  #
1384	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_TEST_FOREGROUND=28
1385	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_TEST_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1386	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_TEST_CONTENT_EXPANSION='> ${P9K_CONTENT} <'
1387	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_CLASSES=(
1388	      # '*prod*'  PROD    # These values are examples that are unlikely
1389	      # '*test*'  TEST    # to match your needs. Customize them as needed.
1390	      '*'       DEFAULT)
1391	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_DEFAULT_FOREGROUND=208
1392	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_DEFAULT_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1393	
1394	  # AWS segment format. The following parameters are available within the expansion.
1395	  #
1396	  # - P9K_AWS_PROFILE  The name of the current AWS profile.
1397	  # - P9K_AWS_REGION   The region associated with the current AWS profile.
1398	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_CONTENT_EXPANSION='${P9K_AWS_PROFILE//\%/%%}${P9K_AWS_REGION:+ ${P9K_AWS_REGION//\%/%%}}'
1399	
1400	  #[ aws_eb_env: aws elastic beanstalk environment (https://aws.amazon.com/elasticbeanstalk/) ]#
1401	  # AWS Elastic Beanstalk environment color.
1402	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_EB_ENV_FOREGROUND=70
1403	  # Custom icon.
1404	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AWS_EB_ENV_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1405	
1406	  ##########[ azure: azure account name (https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cli/azure) ]##########
1407	  # Show azure only when the command you are typing invokes one of these tools.
1408	  # Tip: Remove the next line to always show azure.
1409	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AZURE_SHOW_ON_COMMAND='az|terraform|pulumi|terragrunt'
1410	
1411	  # POWERLEVEL9K_AZURE_CLASSES is an array with even number of elements. The first element
1412	  # in each pair defines a pattern against which the current azure account name gets matched.
1413	  # More specifically, it's P9K_CONTENT prior to the application of context expansion (see below)
1414	  # that gets matched. If you unset all POWERLEVEL9K_AZURE_*CONTENT_EXPANSION parameters,
1415	  # you'll see this value in your prompt. The second element of each pair in
1416	  # POWERLEVEL9K_AZURE_CLASSES defines the account class. Patterns are tried in order. The
1417	  # first match wins.
1418	  #
1419	  # For example, given these settings:
1420	  #
1421	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AZURE_CLASSES=(
1422	  #     '*prod*'  PROD
1423	  #     '*test*'  TEST
1424	  #     '*'       OTHER)
1425	  #
1426	  # If your current azure account is "company_test", its class is TEST because "company_test"
1427	  # doesn't match the pattern '*prod*' but does match '*test*'.
1428	  #
1429	  # You can define different colors, icons and content expansions for different classes:
1430	  #
1431	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AZURE_TEST_FOREGROUND=28
1432	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AZURE_TEST_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1433	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AZURE_TEST_CONTENT_EXPANSION='> ${P9K_CONTENT} <'
1434	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AZURE_CLASSES=(
1435	      # '*prod*'  PROD    # These values are examples that are unlikely
1436	      # '*test*'  TEST    # to match your needs. Customize them as needed.
1437	      '*'         OTHER)
1438	
1439	  # Azure account name color.
1440	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AZURE_OTHER_FOREGROUND=32
1441	  # Custom icon.
1442	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_AZURE_OTHER_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1443	
1444	  ##########[ gcloud: google cloud account and project (https://cloud.google.com/) ]###########
1445	  # Show gcloud only when the command you are typing invokes one of these tools.
1446	  # Tip: Remove the next line to always show gcloud.
1447	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GCLOUD_SHOW_ON_COMMAND='gcloud|gcs|gsutil'
1448	   # Google cloud color.
1449	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GCLOUD_FOREGROUND=32
1450	
1451	  # Google cloud format. Change the value of POWERLEVEL9K_GCLOUD_PARTIAL_CONTENT_EXPANSION and/or
1452	  # POWERLEVEL9K_GCLOUD_COMPLETE_CONTENT_EXPANSION if the default is too verbose or not informative
1453	  # enough. You can use the following parameters in the expansions. Each of them corresponds to the
1454	  # output of `gcloud` tool.
1455	  #
1456	  #   Parameter                | Source
1457	  #   -------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------
1458	  #   P9K_GCLOUD_CONFIGURATION | gcloud config configurations list --format='value(name)'
1459	  #   P9K_GCLOUD_ACCOUNT       | gcloud config get-value account
1460	  #   P9K_GCLOUD_PROJECT_ID    | gcloud config get-value project
1461	  #   P9K_GCLOUD_PROJECT_NAME  | gcloud projects describe $P9K_GCLOUD_PROJECT_ID --format='value(name)'
1462	  #
1463	  # Note: ${VARIABLE//\%/%%} expands to ${VARIABLE} with all occurrences of '%' replaced with '%%'.
1464	  #
1465	  # Obtaining project name requires sending a request to Google servers. This can take a long time
1466	  # and even fail. When project name is unknown, P9K_GCLOUD_PROJECT_NAME is not set and gcloud
1467	  # prompt segment is in state PARTIAL. When project name gets known, P9K_GCLOUD_PROJECT_NAME gets
1468	  # set and gcloud prompt segment transitions to state COMPLETE.
1469	  #
1470	  # You can customize the format, icon and colors of gcloud segment separately for states PARTIAL
1471	  # and COMPLETE. You can also hide gcloud in state PARTIAL by setting
1472	  # POWERLEVEL9K_GCLOUD_PARTIAL_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION and
1473	  # POWERLEVEL9K_GCLOUD_PARTIAL_CONTENT_EXPANSION to empty.
1474	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GCLOUD_PARTIAL_CONTENT_EXPANSION='${P9K_GCLOUD_PROJECT_ID//\%/%%}'
1475	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GCLOUD_COMPLETE_CONTENT_EXPANSION='${P9K_GCLOUD_PROJECT_NAME//\%/%%}'
1476	
1477	  # Send a request to Google (by means of `gcloud projects describe ...`) to obtain project name
1478	  # this often. Negative value disables periodic polling. In this mode project name is retrieved
1479	  # only when the current configuration, account or project id changes.
1480	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GCLOUD_REFRESH_PROJECT_NAME_SECONDS=60
1481	
1482	  # Custom icon.
1483	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GCLOUD_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1484	
1485	  #[ google_app_cred: google application credentials (https://cloud.google.com/docs/authentication/production) ]#
1486	  # Show google_app_cred only when the command you are typing invokes one of these tools.
1487	  # Tip: Remove the next line to always show google_app_cred.
1488	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_SHOW_ON_COMMAND='terraform|pulumi|terragrunt'
1489	
1490	  # Google application credentials classes for the purpose of using different colors, icons and
1491	  # expansions with different credentials.
1492	  #
1493	  # POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_CLASSES is an array with even number of elements. The first
1494	  # element in each pair defines a pattern against which the current kubernetes context gets
1495	  # matched. More specifically, it's P9K_CONTENT prior to the application of context expansion
1496	  # (see below) that gets matched. If you unset all POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_*CONTENT_EXPANSION
1497	  # parameters, you'll see this value in your prompt. The second element of each pair in
1498	  # POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_CLASSES defines the context class. Patterns are tried in order.
1499	  # The first match wins.
1500	  #
1501	  # For example, given these settings:
1502	  #
1503	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_CLASSES=(
1504	  #     '*:*prod*:*'  PROD
1505	  #     '*:*test*:*'  TEST
1506	  #     '*'           DEFAULT)
1507	  #
1508	  # If your current Google application credentials is "service_account deathray-testing x@y.com",
1509	  # its class is TEST because it doesn't match the pattern '* *prod* *' but does match '* *test* *'.
1510	  #
1511	  # You can define different colors, icons and content expansions for different classes:
1512	  #
1513	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_TEST_FOREGROUND=28
1514	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_TEST_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1515	  #   typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_TEST_CONTENT_EXPANSION='$P9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_PROJECT_ID'
1516	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_CLASSES=(
1517	      # '*:*prod*:*'  PROD    # These values are examples that are unlikely
1518	      # '*:*test*:*'  TEST    # to match your needs. Customize them as needed.
1519	      '*'             DEFAULT)
1520	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_DEFAULT_FOREGROUND=32
1521	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_DEFAULT_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1522	
1523	  # Use POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_CONTENT_EXPANSION to specify the content displayed by
1524	  # google_app_cred segment. Parameter expansions are very flexible and fast, too. See reference:
1525	  # http://zsh.sourceforge.net/Doc/Release/Expansion.html#Parameter-Expansion.
1526	  #
1527	  # You can use the following parameters in the expansion. Each of them corresponds to one of the
1528	  # fields in the JSON file pointed to by GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS.
1529	  #
1530	  #   Parameter                        | JSON key file field
1531	  #   ---------------------------------+---------------
1532	  #   P9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_TYPE         | type
1533	  #   P9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_PROJECT_ID   | project_id
1534	  #   P9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_CLIENT_EMAIL | client_email
1535	  #
1536	  # Note: ${VARIABLE//\%/%%} expands to ${VARIABLE} with all occurrences of '%' replaced by '%%'.
1537	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_DEFAULT_CONTENT_EXPANSION='${P9K_GOOGLE_APP_CRED_PROJECT_ID//\%/%%}'
1538	
1539	  ##############[ toolbox: toolbox name (https://github.com/containers/toolbox) ]###############
1540	  # Toolbox color.
1541	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TOOLBOX_FOREGROUND=178
1542	  # Don't display the name of the toolbox if it matches fedora-toolbox-*.
1543	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TOOLBOX_CONTENT_EXPANSION='${P9K_TOOLBOX_NAME:#fedora-toolbox-*}'
1544	  # Custom icon.
1545	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TOOLBOX_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1546	  # Custom prefix.
1547	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TOOLBOX_PREFIX='%fin '
1548	
1549	  ###############################[ public_ip: public IP address ]###############################
1550	  # Public IP color.
1551	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PUBLIC_IP_FOREGROUND=94
1552	  # Custom icon.
1553	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_PUBLIC_IP_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1554	
1555	  ########################[ vpn_ip: virtual private network indicator ]#########################
1556	  # VPN IP color.
1557	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VPN_IP_FOREGROUND=81
1558	  # When on VPN, show just an icon without the IP address.
1559	  # Tip: To display the private IP address when on VPN, remove the next line.
1560	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_VPN_IP_CONTENT_EXPANSION=

1581	  #   P9K_IP_TX_BYTES       | total number of bytes sent

1592

1602	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_BATTERY_LOW_FOREGROUND=160
1603	  # Show battery in green when it's charging or fully charged.

1605	  # Show battery in yellow when it's discharging.

1626	  #
1627	  # The following parameters are accessible within the expansions:
1628	  #
1629	  #   Parameter             | Meaning
1630	  #   ----------------------+---------------
1631	  #   P9K_WIFI_SSID         | service set identifier, a.k.a. network name
1632	  #   P9K_WIFI_LINK_AUTH    | authentication protocol such as "wpa2-psk" or "none"; empty if unknown
1633	  #   P9K_WIFI_LAST_TX_RATE | wireless transmit rate in megabits per second
1634	  #   P9K_WIFI_RSSI         | signal strength in dBm, from -120 to 0
1635	  #   P9K_WIFI_NOISE        | noise in dBm, from -120 to 0
1636	  #   P9K_WIFI_BARS         | signal strength in bars, from 0 to 4 (derived from P9K_WIFI_RSSI and P9K_WIFI_NOISE)
1637

1639	  # Current time color.
1640	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TIME_FOREGROUND=66
1641	  # Format for the current time: 09:51:02. See `man 3 strftime`.
1642	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TIME_FORMAT='%D{%H:%M:%S}'
1643	  # If set to true, time will update when you hit enter. This way prompts for the past
1644	  # commands will contain the start times of their commands as opposed to the default
1645	  # behavior where they contain the end times of their preceding commands.
1646	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TIME_UPDATE_ON_COMMAND=false

1649	  # Custom prefix.

1652	  # Example of a user-defined prompt segment. Function prompt_example will be called on every
1653	  # prompt if `example` prompt segment is added to POWERLEVEL9K_LEFT_PROMPT_ELEMENTS or
1654	  # POWERLEVEL9K_RIGHT_PROMPT_ELEMENTS. It displays an icon and orange text greeting the user.
1655	  #
1656	  # Type `p10k help segment` for documentation and a more sophisticated example.
1657	  function prompt_example() {
1658	    p10k segment -f 208 -i '⭐' -t 'hello, %n'
1659	  }
1660	
1661	  # User-defined prompt segments may optionally provide an instant_prompt_* function. Its job
1662	  # is to generate the prompt segment for display in instant prompt. See
1663	  # https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k#instant-prompt.
1664	  #
1665	  # Powerlevel10k will call instant_prompt_* at the same time as the regular prompt_* function
1666	  # and will record all `p10k segment` calls it makes. When displaying instant prompt, Powerlevel10k
1667	  # will replay these calls without actually calling instant_prompt_*. It is imperative that
1668	  # instant_prompt_* always makes the same `p10k segment` calls regardless of environment. If this
1669	  # rule is not observed, the content of instant prompt will be incorrect.
1670	  #
1671	  # Usually, you should either not define instant_prompt_* or simply call prompt_* from it. If
1672	  # instant_prompt_* is not defined for a segment, the segment won't be shown in instant prompt.
1673	  function instant_prompt_example() {
1674	    # Since prompt_example always makes the same `p10k segment` calls, we can call it from
1675	    # instant_prompt_example. This will give us the same `example` prompt segment in the instant
1676	    # and regular prompts.
1677	    prompt_example
1678	  }
1679	
1680	  # User-defined prompt segments can be customized the same way as built-in segments.
1681	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_EXAMPLE_FOREGROUND=208
1682	  # typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_EXAMPLE_VISUAL_IDENTIFIER_EXPANSION='⭐'
1683	
1684	  # Transient prompt works similarly to the builtin transient_rprompt option. It trims down prompt
1685	  # when accepting a command line. Supported values:
1686	  #
1687	  #   - off:      Don't change prompt when accepting a command line.
1688	  #   - always:   Trim down prompt when accepting a command line.
1689	  #   - same-dir: Trim down prompt when accepting a command line unless this is the first command
1690	  #               typed after changing current working directory.
1691	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_TRANSIENT_PROMPT=always
1692	
1693	  # Instant prompt mode.
1694	  #
1695	  #   - off:     Disable instant prompt. Choose this if you've tried instant prompt and found
1696	  #              it incompatible with your zsh configuration files.
1697	  #   - quiet:   Enable instant prompt and don't print warnings when detecting console output
1698	  #              during zsh initialization. Choose this if you've read and understood
1699	  #              https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k#instant-prompt.
1700	  #   - verbose: Enable instant prompt and print a warning when detecting console output during
1701	  #              zsh initialization. Choose this if you've never tried instant prompt, haven't
1702	  #              seen the warning, or if you are unsure what this all means.
1703	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_INSTANT_PROMPT=verbose
1704	
1705	  # Hot reload allows you to change POWERLEVEL9K options after Powerlevel10k has been initialized.
1706	  # For example, you can type POWERLEVEL9K_BACKGROUND=red and see your prompt turn red. Hot reload
1707	  # can slow down prompt by 1-2 milliseconds, so it's better to keep it turned off unless you
1708	  # really need it.
1709	  typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_DISABLE_HOT_RELOAD=true
1710	
1711	  # If p10k is already loaded, reload configuration.
1712	  # This works even with POWERLEVEL9K_DISABLE_HOT_RELOAD=true.
1713	  (( ! $+functions[p10k] )) || p10k reload
1714	}
1715	
1716	# Tell `p10k configure` which file it should overwrite.
1717	typeset -g POWERLEVEL9K_CONFIG_FILE=${${(%):-%x}:a}

1719	(( ${#p10k_config_opts} )) && setopt ${p10k_config_opts[@]}

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-06-12 04:58:39Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `/Users/grant/.claude/statusline-command.sh`

```text
1	#!/usr/bin/env sh
2	# Claude Code status line — styled after Powerlevel10k lean prompt
3	# Left:  dir  git-branch [git-flags]
4	# Right: model  context%
5	
6	input=$(cat)
7	
8	current_dir=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.workspace.current_dir // .cwd')
9	model=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.model.display_name')
10	used_pct=$(echo "$input" | jq -r '.context_window.used_percentage // empty')
11	
12	# Directory: full path, home-relative (e.g. ~/Documents/foo or ~)
13	home_dir="$HOME"
14	case "$current_dir" in
15	  "$home_dir") dir="~" ;;
16	  "$home_dir"/*) dir="~${current_dir#$home_dir}" ;;
17	  *) dir="$current_dir" ;;
18	esac
19	
20	# Git branch + status flags (skip optional locks)
21	git_branch=$(git -C "$current_dir" branch --show-current 2>/dev/null || true)
22	git_part=""
23	if [ -n "$git_branch" ]; then
24	  git_flags=""
25	  # Staged changes  (+)
26	  staged=$(git -C "$current_dir" diff --cached --name-only 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
27	  [ "$staged" -gt 0 ] && git_flags="${git_flags}+"
28	  # Unstaged changes (!)
29	  unstaged=$(git -C "$current_dir" diff --name-only 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
30	  [ "$unstaged" -gt 0 ] && git_flags="${git_flags}!"
31	  # Untracked files (?)
32	  untracked=$(git -C "$current_dir" ls-files --others --exclude-standard 2>/dev/null | wc -l | tr -d ' ')
33	  [ "$untracked" -gt 0 ] && git_flags="${git_flags}?"
34	  # Ahead/behind
35	  ahead=$(git -C "$current_dir" rev-list --count @{u}..HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
36	  behind=$(git -C "$current_dir" rev-list --count HEAD..@{u} 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
37	  [ "$ahead" -gt 0 ] && git_flags="${git_flags}⇡${ahead}"
38	  [ "$behind" -gt 0 ] && git_flags="${git_flags}⇣${behind}"
39	
40	  if [ -n "$git_flags" ]; then
41	    git_part=" ${git_branch} ${git_flags}"
42	  else
43	    git_part=" ${git_branch}"
44	  fi
45	fi
46	
47	# Context window
48	ctx_part=""
49	if [ -n "$used_pct" ]; then
50	  used_int=$(printf "%.0f" "$used_pct")
51	  ctx_part=" · ctx ${used_int}%"
52	fi
53	
54	# Build output: dim the whole line (status line is already dimmed by Claude Code)
55	printf "%s%s · %s%s\n" "$dir" "$git_part" "$model" "$ctx_part"
56
```
</details></tool-use>
